title: Understand: First Amendment: Religion—Free Exercise Clause
description: Check your understanding of the First Amendment's Free Exercise Clause with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Understand: First Amendment: Religion—Free Exercise Clause

assessment_items.question_content:
**In *Reynolds v. United States* (1879), the Supreme Court ruled that the Free Exercise Clause \_\_\_\_.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: allowed the government to prohibit certain religious practices
allowed the government to regulate certain religious beliefs
prohibited the government from interfering with religious practices
required the establishment of a national religion

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the central issue in *Reynolds v. United States* (1879)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The right of Amish parents to homeschool their children
Whether Native American tribes could use peyote in religious ceremonies
The constitutionality of laws prohibiting polygamy
The tax-exempt status of religious organizations

assessment_items.question_content:
**What were the central issues in *Wisconsin v. Yoder* (1972)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Religious freedom and anti-discrimination laws
Free exercise of religion and compulsory education laws
Establishment of religion and government funding for religious schools
Freedom of speech and regulation of hate speech

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the Supreme Court’s ruling in *Wisconsin v. Yoder* (1972)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Wisconsin could punish Amish parents for refusing to send their children to school past the eighth grade.
The Amish could not claim exemptions from compulsory education laws for religious reasons.
The Free Exercise Clause did not protect religious practices that were contrary to public education laws.
The Free Exercise Clause allowed Amish parents to remove their children from school after eighth grade.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the main issue in *Employment Division v. Smith* (1990)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Religious displays on public property
Laws prohibiting animal sacrifice in religious rituals
Tax exemptions for religious organizations
Religious exemptions from drug laws

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the outcome of *Employment Division v. Smith* (1990)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Supreme Court upheld Native American employees' right to use peyote in their place of employment.
The Supreme Court upheld an agency’s decision to deny unemployment benefits to Native Americans who used peyote during a religious ceremony.
The Supreme Court struck down laws prohibiting the use of peyote in religious rituals as a violation of the Free Exercise Clause.
The Supreme Court held that the government couldn't interfere with religious practices, even if the practices violated neutral laws of general applicability.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the central issue in *Roman Catholic Diocese of Brooklyn v. Cuomo* (2020)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The right of religious organizations to receive government funding
The tax-exempt status of religious institutions
The constitutionality of restrictions on religious gatherings during a pandemic
The prohibition of religious symbols in public schools

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the Supreme Court rule *Roman Catholic Diocese of Brooklyn v. Cuomo* (2020)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Court upheld COVID-19 restrictions on religious gatherings, prioritizing public health over religious freedom.
The Court struck down COVID-19 restrictions on religious gatherings as violations of the Free Exercise Clause.
The Court ruled that religious groups lacked standing to challenge COVID-19 restrictions on gatherings.
The Court held that the government may never restrict religious gatherings during a pandemic.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did *Reynolds v. United States* (1879) influence the interpretation of the Free Exercise Clause over time?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It established a broad protection for all religious practices, exempting them from government regulation.
It allowed the government to regulate some religious practices, if those practices violated laws that were neutral and generally applicable to all.
It provided heightened protection for minority religious groups, allowing them to practice their beliefs without interference.
It clarified that the Free Exercise Clause fully protects both religious beliefs and religious practices that conflict with local laws.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement best describes the protections guaranteed under the Free Exercise Clause of the First Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The right to believe in any religion you choose or no religion at all
The right to move anywhere without government interference
The prohibition of a government-sponsored religion
The prohibition of restrictions on the publication of news

assessment_items.question_content:
**In *Cantwell v. Connecticut* (1940), the Supreme Court wrote that the Free Exercise Clause embraces “two concepts.” What are those TWO concepts?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The freedom to believe
The freedom to act
The freedom to inform others
The freedom to fund

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which early Supreme Court case incorporated the Free Exercise Clause and applied it to state governments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Cantwell v. Connecticut* (1940)
*Wisconsin v. Yoder* (1972)
*Employment Division v. Smith* (1990)
*Roman Catholic Diocese of Brooklyn v. Cuomo* (2020)
